text:
# Page 1^page=1
Access Control for Asset Administration Shell Applications Ornella Mboudia*, Sebastian Heppner, Torben Miny, Tobias Kleinert Chair of Information and Automation Systems RWTH Aachen University Aachen, Germany {o.mboudia, s.heppner, t.miny, kleinert}@iat.rwth-aachen.de Abstract—The Asset Administration Shell is emerging as the standardized digital twin representation for Industrie 4.0 assets, enabling interoperability across organizational and technical boundaries. However, existing access control mechanisms fail to satisfy the Asset Administration Shell’s needs for ﬁne-grained, context-aware, cross-domain security. This paper proposes an access control concept tailored to the Asset Administration Shell, combining Role Based Access Control and Attribute Based Access Control to meet a set of ten key requirements derived from standards, literature, and expert interviews. Our approach fol- lows a technology-neutral architecture, with a concrete prototype implementation using Keycloak as identity provider, Envoy for request interception, and Open Policy Agent for centralized policy based decision-making; thereby separating security con- cerns from application logic. An evaluation against the deﬁned requirements shows that this prototype meets nine of the ten requirements. Overall, this concept lays the groundwork for future development of secure, scalable, and ﬂexible access control solutions in industrial Asset Administration Shell deployments. Index Terms—Access Control, Authorization, Asset Adminis- tration Shell, Security, Industrie 4.0 I. INTRODUCTION The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) is the standardized representation of an asset, e.g. anything with a real or per- ceived value under the custodial duties of an organization [1]. It was introduced 2016 in the German DIN SPEC 91345 [2] and internationalized in IEC 63278 series [3]. Current development of the AAS speciﬁcation is led by the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA) 1. Its adoption is rapidly gaining momentum in initiatives such as the BaSys projects, Manufacturing-X, and the Digital Product Passport 4.0. Despite this growing popularity, current implementations of the AAS often fall short in integrating robust, state-of-the-art security mechanisms. Security, particularly Access Control (AC), has emerged as a critical topic in both industrial standardization efforts and academic research. Key stakeholders such as Platform Industrie 4.0 and the IDTA have identiﬁed this gap and initiated work toward harmonized security frameworks. To address this, we propose a novel, modular AC concept tailored to the AAS, which decouples security concerns from core Industrie 4.0 (I4.0) components. A key advantage of this decoupled design is allowing our concept to be inte- grated on top of existing AAS services, enabling seamless 1https://industrialdigitaltwin.org/ enhancement of security for systems already in production. Our approach combines Role Based Access Control (RBAC) and Attribute Based Access Control (ABAC) to fulﬁll ten key requirements derived from standards, academic literature, and expert interviews. It follows a technology-neutral architecture, with a proof-of-concept open-source implementation, inte- grated with the Eclipse BaSyx Python SDK 2, leveraging Key- cloak for identity management, Envoy for request interception, and Open Policy Agent (OPA) for centralized, policy-based decision-making. This design promotes reusability, scalability, and maintainability while enabling secure AAS integration. An evaluation demonstrates that the prototype satisﬁes nine out of ten identiﬁed requirements. This paper is structured as follows: Section II presents Access Control Models (ACMs) models from the literature and provides an overview of the current state of AC in the context of the AAS, covering standardization efforts, academic research, and implementations in leading AAS software solu- tions. Section III deﬁnes ten key requirements derived from the state-of-the-art review and three expert interviews. Section IV introduces our novel, technology-independent architecture for AC, followed by a concrete, open-source proof-of-concept im- plementation in Section V. Section VI evaluates the proposed concept and implementation against the deﬁned requirements, and Section VII concludes with a summary and outlook. II. STATE OF THE ART According to [4], conﬁdentiality, integrity, and availability are the three key security objectives. Conﬁdentiality focuses on protecting information from unauthorized disclosure, which is primarily achieved through AC mechanisms. However, AC also indirectly contributes to ensuring integrity and availability by preventing unauthorized actions that could compromise data accuracy or system uptime. In this section, we will present the current state of ACMs, the advancements made so far in AC for the AAS, and relevant existing publications addressing AC for the AAS. A. Access Control Models One of the ﬁrst steps in implementing AC is selecting an appropriate ACM. A wide range of ACMs exists in the 2https://github.com/eclipse-basyx/basyx-python-sdk

# Page 2^page=2
literature, each designed to formulate and implement secu- rity requirements for enforcing access policies [5]. ACMs include, among others, Discretionary Access Control (DAC), Mandatory Access Control (MAC), RBAC, and ABAC. These are referred to as traditional ACMs and serve as the foundation for hybrid models, which have been developed to address more speciﬁc use cases [6]. Hybrid ACMs have been emerging over time, typically combining two or more traditional models or extending them. In this work, the focus is placed on RBAC and ABAC, as they are particularly relevant to the implemen- tation of the AC approach presented here. The following part therefore provides a brief overview of these two models. a) Role Based Access Control: The RBAC model deﬁnes access to resources based on roles (see Figure 1a). Permissions are assigned to roles, which are then assigned to users, making access rights task-dependent and aligned with an individual’s position within the organization. A key prerequisite for im- plementing RBAC is therefore a precise assignment of every subject to one or more roles [7]. RBAC supports the implementation of important security principles such as “Least Privilege” and “Separation of Duty”. According to the Least Privilege principle, users are granted only the permissions necessary to perform their tasks, thus minimizing the potential impact of intentional or accidental damage. Although strictly enforcing Least Privilege can be challenging in practice, RBAC provides a structured frame- work to achieve it, especially in complex organizational envi- ronments. “Separation of Duty” ensures that critical tasks are divided among multiple individuals so that no single person possesses all the permissions required to complete those tasks [8]. To enhance manageability and security, RBAC can be extended with hierarchical roles and constrained separation-of-duty mechanisms. In hierarchical RBAC, parent roles automatically inherit all permissions of their child roles, which reduces the number of distinct roles and assignments but presupposes a clear organizational structure and carries the risk of over- privileging users [7]. Constrained RBAC further reﬁnes this model by introducing two forms of separation of duty: static separation of duty prevents users from ever being assigned to conﬂicting roles by enforcing exclusion rules and managing relationship cardinality at assignment time, while dynamic separation of duty allows potentially conﬂicting roles to be assigned but ensures that only non-conﬂicting roles can be activated simultaneously within a user session [8]. b) Attribute Based Access Control: In the ABAC model, resource access is determined by the attributes of both the resource and the subject, environmental conditions, and ad- ditional rules derived from those attributes and conditions. Attributes are properties (e.g., a user’s name or role, or the resource’s classiﬁcation), and environmental conditions deﬁne the context of an access request (e.g., time, date, or location). Each resource is assigned attributes describing its properties, which may refer to part of the resource or the entire resource. For each resource, policies deﬁning access conditions for subjects must be created. These policies specify who may access which resource under which conditions and in which context, following the documented or procedural rules that deﬁne how business processes and allowed actions are handled within the organization [9]. ABAC supports the deﬁnition of access policies for an unlimited number of subjects, even those unknown beforehand and enables ﬁne-grained AC. However, implementing ABAC at an organizational level can be complex due to the number of components required (e.g., assignment and management of attributes, policies, and enforcement points) [9]. An access ﬂow of the ABAC model is illustrated in Figure 1b as well and will be described in more detail later in this work. (a) RBAC derived from [7], [8] (b) ABAC derived from [9] Fig. 1: Overview of RBAC and ABAC mechanisms. B. Progress in AC for the AAS Various researchers and standardization institutions, such as the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA), have proposed

# Page 3^page=3
approaches to implement AC for the AAS. In this subsection, we review and evaluate these approaches. a) AAS AC in standardization organizations: In Novem- ber 2019, version 2.0 of the AAS metamodel introduced, for the ﬁrst time, an AC metamodel based on ABAC [10] [11]. By October 2020, a sub-working group of Platform Industrie 4.0’s “Security of Networked Systems” published the “Secure Download Service” discussion paper, which fo- cused on secure cross-company authentication using X509 certiﬁcates [12] [11]. In the same year, these approaches were implemented as open-source proofs of concept using an AAS server 3, an extended OpenID server 4, and the AASX Package Explorer 5. In this implementation, X.509-based authentication and ﬁne-grained ABAC were realized using a security sub- model, which applied the previously proposed AC metamodel to protect entire AAS, submodels and submodel elements or individual properties [11]. In April 2021, the Task Force “Secure AAS” published “What is the Asset Administration Shell from a techni- cal perspective?”, clarifying the multiple meanings of the term “AAS” (e. g., metamodel, instance data, serialization, Application Programming Interface (API), or server) [11] [13]. Around the same time, this task force also began testing Veriﬁable Credentials as an alternative to X509 certiﬁcate chains, implementing another proof of concept6 using Key- cloak’s Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI) extension [11]. Subsequent ZVEI showcases regarding the DPP40, such as control cabinet7, electro installation, and grid8 , highlighted distributed AAS infrastructures, usage policy negotiation, and data sovereignty. Additionally, they addressed performance challenges for large-scale data interaction, resulting in sim- pliﬁed, faster usage policy negotiation methods [11]. In early 2023, the same Task Force showed how the Eclipse Data Space Connector (EDC), employed in the Tractus-X project, can authenticate dataspace participants and manage usage policy agreements for ﬂexible, interoperable scenarios [11]. Based on these ﬁndings the task Force “Security” of the working group “OpenTechnology” in the IDTA is developing the “Speciﬁcation of the Asset Administration Shell – Part 4: Security” which describes a common grammar and JSON schema for sharing access rules [11]. IEC 63278-3 [14], titled “Asset Administration Shell for Industrial Applications – Part 3: Security Provisions for Asset Administration Shells”, is an international standard currently under development by IEC Technical Committee 65, Working Group 24. This part aims to deﬁne how integrity, authenticity, and conﬁdentiality are realized within the AAS informa- tion model, ensuring secure and interoperable information exchange among stakeholders in value chains. It reﬁnes the 3https://github.com/eclipse-aaspe/server 4https://github.com/admin-shell-io/aasx-IdentityServer4 5https://github.com/eclipse-aaspe/package-explorer 6https://admin-shell-io.com/screencasts/security/Update 6 AAS Security with SSI IDunion Lissi.mp4 7https://pcf.dpp40-2-v2.industrialdigitaltwin.org/pcf2 8https://energy.dpp40-5-v3.industrialdigitaltwin.org/pcf2 security requirements introduced in Part 1 and complements the structural deﬁnitions of Part 2 by deﬁning a dedicated security information model. The activities undertaken so far by standardization orga- nizations such as Platform Industrie 4.0 and the IDTA have signiﬁcantly advanced the understanding of AC implementa- tion for the AAS. In particular, the provided showcases and proofs of concept, together with the upcoming speciﬁcation that will help ensure interoperable exchange of access rules among AAS stakeholders. However, all current proposals of the organization follow a similar approach, and there is no al- ternative AC approaches that might facilitate easier integration for companies. b) AAS AC in academic literature: The most relevant publications addressing AC for the AAS are summarized below, ordered by descending relevance. [15] highlights the need for an AAS provider to deﬁne AC mechanisms for multiple roles and individual resources. This implies that AAS servers must be secured through appropriate authentication and authorization measures. The publication explains that AAS servers may provide both public and restricted data. To access restricted data, a user must ﬁrst authenticate with an Identity Provider (IP) and obtain a JSON Web Token (JWT) 9. The server or an API gateway can then verify the token’s signature. If the veriﬁcation is successful, the AAS server proceeds with the authorization process. This authorization is based on the ABAC model and is carried out in two stages: The ﬁrst stage checks authorization against the API route, and the second stage evaluates access to the requested resource. [15] adopts an approach that stores access rules within the AAS format using an AC submodel. However, the complexity and inefﬁciency of this method have already been highlighted by [16]. [16] discusses two options to integrate AC into the AAS ecosystem. One option is to use a submodel template, as proposed by the IDTA, which would be incorporated into each AAS instance. The authors evaluate this method as inadequate, as each submodel of the AAS require individual AC, and the AC submodel would have to reference individual submodels of the AAS. This increases the risk of errors and introduces unnecessary complexity. The alternative approach expands the metamodel of the AAS with data governance aspects such as AC. This involves introducing abstract classes for user metadata and authorization, which are inherited by the AAS and its submodels. In this way, the AAS inherently includes AC functionality. While this second approach addresses the limitations of the security submodel, it introduces other chal- lenges. Extending the metamodel increases the complexity of the AAS speciﬁcation itself and limit interoperability with existing tools and systems that are based on the standard AAS metamodel. Furthermore, embedding access control deeply into the AAS model conﬂicts with the principle of separation of concerns, as it mixes data representation with security logic. 9https://jwt.io/

# Page 4^page=4
Earlier [17] addresses the integration of AC mechanisms within AAS by leveraging the Open Platform Communica- tions Uniﬁed Architecture (OPC UA) standard. They identify seven requirements for enforcing authorization in the AAS. These requirements include support at both the submodel and individual element level, view-based authorization (for exam- ple, aligned with different device lifecycle phases), dynamic permission updates, support for multiple security domains, inheritance of access rules from parent AAS resources to their children, and context-aware attributes such as time-of- day constraints. The authors propose a method to implement authorization in AAS by utilizing OPC UA’s built-in security features. They suggest modeling AAS elements as OPC UA nodes, allowing the use of OPC UA’s RBAC to manage per- missions. While this approach effectively leverages OPC UA’s mature security infrastructure to address AC for the AAS, its reliance on OPC UA as the underlying communication and modeling framework limits ﬂexibility in heterogeneous environments and alternative use cases. c) AAS AC in leading software implementations: The BaSyx Java SDK10 team has implemented conﬁgurable AC for the AAS through a combination of authentication and role- based authorization. The authorization supports two modes: GrantedAuthority and SimpleRBAC. In the GrantedAuthority mode, each incoming HTTP request carries a Spring Secu- rity 11 Authentication object, which includes granted authorities derived from the JWT bearer token found in the authoriza- tion header. This token is validated against an authoriza- tion server such as Keycloak, and its claims are mapped directly into GrantedAuthority entries. These entries deﬁne permissions for accessing whole AAS, submodels or reg- istry server. Furthermore, the SDK provides extension points via interfaces such as ISubjectInformationProvider and IGrantedAuthorityAuthenticator, allowing for customized authority retrieval and integration into diverse authentication setups. This ﬂexibility allows adaptation of the AC mechanism to different token providers or authorization schemes. The SimpleRBAC mode offers greater ﬂexibility by enabling the conﬁguration of individual roles and rules for accessing speciﬁc resources, such as individual submodel elements. This mode leverages OAuth2 12 with JWT access tokens and has been validated using Keycloak as well. By default, the JWT conveys only the roles assigned to the requester. Access decisions are then governed by externally deﬁned rules, which consist of tuples specifying role, action, and resource. This approach allows further customization through the implemen- tation of interfaces such as “ISubjectInformationProvider”, as well, and “IRoleAuthenticator”, which deﬁne how roles are obtained. Additionally, the evaluation of access rules can be adapted by implementing the “IRbacRuleChecker” interface, enabling ﬂexible integration of different rule sources. 10https://wiki.basyx.org/en/latest/content/user documentation/basyx components/v1/general conﬁguration/security/authorization.html 11https://spring.io/projects/spring-security 12https://oauth.net/2/ As the BaSyx Java SDK employs only an RBAC approach, its support for ﬁne-grained and context-dependent AC remains limited. III. AC REQUIREMENTS FOR AN AAS SERVER Based on the ﬁndings reported in [18], the structure of the AAS deﬁned in [19], and insights from three interviews with experts who are actively involved in the development and advancement of AAS technologies, we derived a set of requirements for AC on an AAS and its environments: R-01 Identiﬁcation and authentication: Before granting access to an AAS, users must prove their identity via secure methods (e.g., passwords, digital certiﬁcates, or multi-factor authentication). This ensures that only veriﬁed and trustworthy entities are allowed to interact with the system. R-02 Role-based user management: The system should support the creation, assignment, and management of user roles. Each role aggregates a predeﬁned set of permissions, thereby simplifying the administra- tion of access rights and ensuring that users can only perform actions aligned with their designated responsibilities. R-03 Linking with other AC management systems: The AC mechanism should be capable of integrating with existing Enterprise Identity and Access Management (IAM) systems. This linkage facilitates centralized policy enforcement, consistent credential manage- ment, and streamlined user provisioning across mul- tiple systems. R-04 Least privilege principle: Every user and process should be granted only the minimal set of permis- sions required to perform their tasks. This approach minimizes the potential damage from a compromised account or accidental misuse and helps to contain security breaches. R-05 Fine-grained AC: The AC system should allow de- tailed speciﬁcation of permissions at various levels, such as per feature, data element, or operation within the AAS. This level of control enables tailored se- curity policies that can differentiate between various users’ needs. R-06 Dynamic AC modeling: The system must be able to adapt AC decisions dynamically based on contextual attributes (e.g., time, location, current threats) as well as changes in users’ roles or environmental condi- tions. This ensures that security measures remain effective under varying operational scenarios. R-07 State-based AC: Access permissions should con- sider the current state or conﬁguration of the AAS. For instance, certain operations may only be al- lowed in speciﬁc operational modes (such as during normal operation versus maintenance mode), which

# Page 5^page=5
contributes to a more resilient and context-aware security posture. R-08 Support of dual consent: For sensitive or critical operations, the system should require authorization from at least two independent parties. This dual- consent mechanism reduces the risk of unilateral actions that could compromise system security or integrity. R-09 Audit: Conﬁgurable audit logging should be im- plemented to record all access events, permission changes, and other security-relevant activities. Such comprehensive logging supports accountability, fa- cilitates forensic investigations, and is essential for compliance with regulatory requirements. R-10 Reuse of access rules: The AC policies should be modular and reusable across various components or instances of the AAS. Reusability promotes consis- tency in security measures, reduces administrative overhead, and simpliﬁes the process of scaling poli- cies to new system elements. IV. AC CONCEPT FOR AAS APPLICATIONS This section describes our AC architecture proposal for AAS Applications (Figure 2) using the combination of RBAC and ABAC in an attribute-centric AC scheme [20]. The protected domain encompasses all AAS components: servers, registry, discovery services, and their hosted resources. To simplify user interaction, the design employs single-sign-on: once a subject (user or AAS client application) authenticates with the external Identity Provider and obtains a signed JWT access token, that token can be presented to any AAS endpoint without re- authentication. Each incoming request is ﬁrst intercepted by a Policy Enforcement Point (PEP), which extracts the token’s claims and forwards them, along with the requested resource identiﬁer, to a Policy Decision Point (PDP). The PDP evaluates the request by matching token-derived attributes (roles, user or device properties) and environmental attributes (such as time or asset state) against the active policy rules stored in the Policy Repository through the Policy Administration Point (PAP); if needed, the PDP may also query a Policy Information Point (PIP) for additional context. If the PDP returns “Allow”, the PEP proxies the request to the target AAS application; otherwise it immediately returns an HTTP “401 Unauthorized” response. By terminating unauthorized requests at the PEP, this architecture shields the AAS applications from unwanted load (e.g., Denial-of-Service (DoS) attempts) and centralizes all policy logic outside the application code. In the event of a DoS attack, the load is primarily directed at the PEP rather than the actual resource servers. When deploying a robust PEP with built-in and easily conﬁgurable protection mechanisms (e.g., rate limiting, timeouts, and connection limits), such attacks are mitigated efﬁciently. This approach requires signiﬁcantly less effort than implementing equivalent protections individually for each resource server. V. IMPLEMENTATION OF THE ASSET ADMINISTRATION SHELLS AC CONCEPT Envoy13 and OPA 14 form the enforcement backbone of our prototype. Envoy is a side-car proxy that hosts a ﬁlter chain that handles both authentication and authorization. Incoming AAS requests ﬁrst hit Envoy’s OAuth2 ﬁlter, which delegates login and token exchange to Keycloak 15, then its external authorization (ext authz) ﬁlter, which calls OPA over Google Remote Procedure Call (gRPC) to obtain an allow or deny decision. OPA is a general-purpose policy engine that decouples policy logic from application code. Policies are written in the declarative Rego language; at runtime Envoy submits each request’s JWT claims and contextual metadata to OPA, which evaluates policy rules and returns a decision in milliseconds16. Together, Envoy ofﬂoads all transport- and protocol-level security concerns, while OPA centralizes ﬁne-grained, context-aware AC, allowing the AAS applications themselves to remain unchanged and focused solely on domain logic. Figure 3 shows the interaction workﬂow between Envoy, Keycloak, and OPA. A proof-of-concept implementation is published as part of the Eclipse BaSyx Python SDK 17. VI. EVALUATION In this section we evaluate our prototype implementation of the AC concept for AAS application against the requirements from Section III. The prototype enforces identiﬁcation and authentication (R- 01) by fronting all AAS endpoints with Envoy’s OAuth2 ﬁlter. Any request lacking a valid JWT is rejected. As a result, only authenticated principals ever reach the AAS applications. Furthermore, Keycloak as an IAM supports integration with external IPs (R-03) such as Active Directory (AD). The combination of ABAC and RBAC fulﬁlls the require- ment role-based user management (R-02) and ﬁne-grained AC (R-05). The roles are deﬁned in the Keycloak client and embedded as JWT claims. Most of the requirements are satisﬁed by using OPA and Envoy. For example, the principle of least privilege (R-04) is inherently supported by the OPA policy design: actions are denied by default, and only explicitly authorized actions, as deﬁned in the Rego ﬁle 17, are allowed. Any operation not covered by a rule is thus automatically rejected. Dynamic AC modeling (R-06) and state-based control (R-07) are enabled through OPA’s support for runtime attributes. Although the provided example is static, the same mechanism can incor- porate time, asset state, or external context via OPA’s PIP queries, allowing policies to adapt dynamically. Audit logging (R-09) is provided at two levels: Envoy access logs capture all requests, and OPA decision logs record each allow/deny 13https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/v1.33.0/ 14https://www.openpolicyagent.org/docs/latest/ 15https://www.keycloak.org/ 16https://www.openpolicyagent.org/docs/latest/policy-performance/ 17https://github.com/rwth-iat/basyx-python-sdk/tree/Security

# Page 6^page=6
Fig. 2: Technology-Neutral AC Architecture for the AAS decision with full token claims and timestamps. Finally, reuse of access rules (R-10) is achieved by centralizing all Rego ﬁles in the policies/ folder. The same policy set can be mounted into every AAS application. Therefore, access rules need be written only once and apply uniformly across all applications. If required, the policies may also be shared with additional stakeholders. Dual consent (R-08) is the sole limitation in the proposed prototype as the current policy framework issues a single bi- nary decision. Implementing two-party approval would require either composite policies (requiring two distinct roles) or an external orchestration step, which is not included by default. In summary, the prototype fully satisﬁes nine of ten require- ments. The only missing feature can be addressed in future work by extending OPA policies or adding a multi-signature workﬂow component. VII. SUMMARY AND OUTLOOK This paper presented a comprehensive review of the current state of AC for the AAS across standardization efforts, aca- demic research, and leading software implementations. Based on this review and insights from three expert interviews, ten key requirements for an AC concept tailored to the AAS were derived. We presented a novel, technology-independent architecture that cleanly separates security concerns from AAS application logic. This architecture was realized in a proof-of- concept implementation using state-of-the-art tools including Envoy, OPA, and Keycloak. Evaluation showed that the imple- mentation meets nine out of the ten deﬁned requirements, with only dual consent (R-08) currently unmet. A key advantage of our approach is its decoupled design, which allows it to be integrated on top of existing AAS services, enabling seamless enhancement of security for systems already in production. In future work, we plan to forward logs from OPA and

# Page 7^page=7
Fig. 3: AC Sequence Diagram Envoy to an external audit tool to improve traceability and monitoring. Beyond AC, it is essential to address broader aspects of AAS security, including ensuring data integrity through mech- anisms like digital signatures. Additionally, the encryption of sensitive data within AASs presents a critical area for further research. Finally, once the IDTA, as the standardization body of the AAS, publishes Part 4 “Security” of the speciﬁcation, its content should be incorporated into our concept to ensure alignment with the standards. VIII. ACKNOWLEDGMENTS The authors would like to thank the experts who participated in the interviews for this research. Your practical knowledge and contributions have enriched this research on AC for the AAS.
